title: Fourth Amendment — Unreasonable Seizure (Stop)
summary: An investigative stop requires reasonable, articulable suspicion of criminal activity. Mere presence in a high-crime area or refusal to answer is insufficient.
source: U.S. Const. amend. IV; Terry v. Ohio, 392 U.S. 1 (1968)
subject: officer

title: Terry Frisk — Limited Pat-Down
summary: A protective frisk is permissible only when officer has reasonable suspicion the subject is armed and dangerous. Search must be limited to outer clothing for weapons; cannot extend to general evidence search ('plain feel' doctrine narrow).
source: Terry v. Ohio, 392 U.S. 1 (1968); Minnesota v. Dickerson, 508 U.S. 366 (1993)
subject: officer

title: Fourth Amendment — Warrantless Search
summary: Searches generally require a warrant supported by probable cause. Exceptions: consent, exigent circumstances, search incident to arrest, plain view, automobile exception. Each is narrowly construed.
source: U.S. Const. amend. IV; Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347 (1967)
subject: officer

title: Fourth Amendment — Excessive Force
summary: Use of force must be objectively reasonable under totality of circumstances. Three Graham factors: severity of crime, immediate threat posed by suspect, active resistance or flight. Evaluated from perspective of reasonable officer on scene.
source: Graham v. Connor, 490 U.S. 386 (1989)
subject: officer

title: Fourth Amendment — Deadly Force
summary: Deadly force against a fleeing suspect is permissible only when officer has probable cause to believe suspect poses significant threat of death or serious physical injury to officer or others. Cannot be used merely to prevent escape of non-dangerous suspect.
source: Tennessee v. Garner, 471 U.S. 1 (1985)
subject: officer

title: Arrest Without Probable Cause
summary: A custodial arrest requires probable cause — facts and circumstances sufficient for a reasonable person to believe a crime has been committed by the suspect.
source: U.S. Const. amend. IV; Beck v. Ohio, 379 U.S. 89 (1964)
subject: officer

title: Fifth Amendment — Miranda Warning
summary: Before custodial interrogation, suspects must be informed: (1) right to remain silent, (2) anything said may be used against them, (3) right to attorney, (4) attorney appointed if cannot afford one. Statements without warning are presumptively inadmissible.
source: Miranda v. Arizona, 384 U.S. 436 (1966)
subject: officer

title: Fifth Amendment — Coerced Confession
summary: Confessions obtained through physical or psychological coercion are inadmissible. Includes threats, prolonged questioning without rest, deprivation, and explicit promises of leniency that overbear free will.
source: U.S. Const. amend. V; Brown v. Mississippi, 297 U.S. 278 (1936)
subject: officer

title: Sixth Amendment — Right to Counsel
summary: Once a suspect invokes right to counsel, all interrogation must cease until counsel is present or suspect reinitiates. Cannot ignore invocation or use trickery to reopen questioning.
source: U.S. Const. amend. VI; Gideon v. Wainwright, 372 U.S. 335 (1963); Edwards v. Arizona, 451 U.S. 477 (1981)
subject: officer

title: First Amendment — Protected Speech
summary: Citizens have the right to verbally challenge or criticize police, including with profanity. Non-threatening speech alone cannot be the basis for arrest. 'Fighting words' doctrine narrowly applied.
source: U.S. Const. amend. I; City of Houston v. Hill, 482 U.S. 451 (1987)
subject: officer

title: First Amendment — Right to Record Police
summary: Citizens have a First Amendment right to record police performing their duties in public. Order to stop recording or seizure of phone without warrant is generally unconstitutional.
source: U.S. Const. amend. I; Glik v. Cunniffe, 655 F.3d 78 (1st Cir. 2011); Fields v. City of Philadelphia, 862 F.3d 353 (3d Cir. 2017)
subject: officer

title: Equal Protection — Racial Profiling
summary: Selective enforcement based on race or ethnicity violates Equal Protection Clause. Stops, searches, or arrests motivated by race require strict scrutiny and rarely survive.
source: U.S. Const. amend. XIV; Whren v. United States, 517 U.S. 806 (1996) (limits)
subject: officer

title: Duty to De-escalate
summary: Officers should attempt de-escalation tactics — verbal calming, distance, time, communication — before applying force when feasible. Failure to attempt de-escalation aggravates excessive force findings.
source: DOJ Consent Decrees (Baltimore 2017, Chicago 2019); PERF Guiding Principles on Use of Force (2016)
subject: officer

title: Officer Identification Requirement
summary: Officers should identify themselves by name, agency, and badge number when requested by a citizen, absent tactical exigency. Concealment of identification during force is procedural violation.
source: Department policies (most major PDs); 42 U.S.C. § 1983 accountability principles
subject: officer

title: Duty to Render Medical Aid
summary: Deliberate indifference to serious medical needs of arrestees or detainees violates the Constitution. Officers must call EMS or render aid when subject shows signs of distress, injury, or medical emergency.
source: Estelle v. Gamble, 429 U.S. 97 (1976); 8th and 14th Amendments
subject: officer

title: Criminal Threats Against Officer
summary: A citizen who makes credible threats of death or great bodily injury against a federal officer or family member commits a federal crime. State analogues criminalize true threats against any peace officer.
source: 18 U.S.C. § 115 (federal); state penal codes (e.g., Cal. Penal Code § 422)
subject: citizen

title: Assault on a Peace Officer
summary: Forcibly assaulting, resisting, opposing, impeding, intimidating, or interfering with a federal officer engaged in official duties is a federal felony. State laws criminalize assault and battery on peace officers similarly.
source: 18 U.S.C. § 111 (federal); state codes (e.g., Cal. Penal Code § 245(c))
subject: citizen

title: Resisting Arrest / Obstruction
summary: Willfully resisting, delaying, or obstructing a peace officer in discharge of duties is a misdemeanor. Mere verbal protest is NOT resistance — physical non-compliance is required (tensing, pulling away, going limp).
source: State penal codes (e.g., Cal. Penal Code § 148(a)(1); N.Y. Penal Law § 205.30)
subject: citizen

title: Fleeing or Evading by Vehicle
summary: Willfully fleeing or attempting to elude a peace officer in a motor vehicle after a lawful signal to stop is a criminal offence. Elevated to felony when reckless or causes injury.
source: State vehicle codes (e.g., Cal. Veh. Code § 2800.1; Tex. Trans. Code § 545.421); federal 18 U.S.C. § 758
subject: citizen

title: Obstruction of Justice
summary: Destroying evidence, providing false identity to law enforcement, or knowingly aiding a suspect's flight constitutes obstruction. Includes lying about identity to officer during lawful stop in states with stop-and-identify statutes.
source: 18 U.S.C. §§ 1501–1521; state analogues
subject: citizen
